message.content:
The salt spray at Blackwood Point didn’t just wet you; it claimed you. For Elias Thorne, it had been claiming his life for thirty years. He was a man carved from driftwood and silence, his skin weathered to the texture of old rope, his world defined by the rhythmic sweep of the lens and the crash of the Atlantic against the jagged teeth of the coast.

It was a Tuesday, gray and biting, when the bottle washed up.

It wasn’t a pristine glass cylinder with a rolled parchment inside. It was a shard of blue sea-glass, jagged and frosted by decades of tumbling in the surf. Elias picked it up, his rough thumb tracing the smooth edge. It felt heavy, denser than glass should be. He turned it over in the light of the lantern room. Inside the hollow core of the glass, trapped in a bubble of air that hadn’t moved since the Victorian era, was a tiny, intricate mechanism.

It wasn’t a note. It was a key.

Elias frowned. He had seen messages before—pleas for help, confessions of love, drunken rants. But he had never seen a key encased in glass. He took the shard back to his quarters, the small, wood-paneled room that smelled of pipe tobacco and damp wool. He placed the glass on his desk, next to a stack of unread logbooks.

That night, the wind howled like a banshee. The lighthouse beam cut through the storm, a solitary eye watching over the dark. Elias couldn’t sleep. The image of the key in the glass haunted him. He picked up the shard again, holding it up to the light. As the beam from the lighthouse swept past his window, the light refracted through the glass, projecting a tiny, distorted shadow onto the wall behind his desk.
